engagement_title: Route Optimization for Regional LTL Carrier

company_profile.business_model: Regional less-than-truckload carrier operating 38 distribution hubs across 6 states

problem_statement: The carrier dispatched routes manually using a combination of dispatcher experience and a legacy TMS that had not been updated since 2014. Route planners spent 3–4 hours per day on manual adjustments to handle exceptions, weather, and driver availability. Fuel costs had risen 18% year-over-year while on-time delivery rates had dropped from 91% to 87% as the network grew.
solution_summary: Tenex built an ML-based route optimisation engine ingesting GPS telemetry, delivery time windows, driver shift constraints, and historical traffic patterns from the prior 18 months. The model used XGBoost regression to predict optimal route sequences and flag at-risk deliveries 4 hours before estimated late. It was deployed as a REST API integrated into the existing TMS, surfacing ranked route suggestions to dispatchers in the planning interface.

primary_metric.label: Fuel Cost Reduction
primary_metric.baseline: $2.1M annual fuel spend across 420 active vehicles
primary_metric.outcome: $1.8M annual fuel spend — $294K annual cost avoidance

secondary_metrics.label: On-time delivery rate
secondary_metrics.value: Improved from 87% to 94%

secondary_metrics.label: Dispatcher planning time
secondary_metrics.value: Reduced from 3.5 hours/day to 45 minutes/day

secondary_metrics.label: Late delivery alerts acted on
secondary_metrics.value: 78% of at-risk deliveries rerouted before SLA breach

results.measurement_basis: Compared to same 4-month period in prior year, adjusted for fuel price index

engagement_details.client_team_involvement: 1 client data engineer for TMS API access, 2 dispatchers as subject matter experts during model validation

tech_stack.ml_approach: XGBoost regression for route scoring; rule-based late delivery alerting layer

lessons_learned: Dispatcher buy-in was the critical path — spend week 2 doing side-by-side sessions before showing model outputs. The model accuracy at launch was good enough but dispatcher trust took 6 weeks to build.

engagement_title: Carrier Scoring for B2B Supply Chain SaaS

company_profile.business_model: B2B SaaS platform serving supply chain managers at mid-market shippers; ~300 client accounts

problem_statement: Supply chain managers using the platform selected carriers manually from a vendor list, relying on static rate cards and personal relationships. The platform had 18 months of shipment performance data across 80+ carriers but no tooling to surface it systematically. Clients were leaving for competitors that offered carrier recommendations, and the product team had no ML capability.

engagement_title: Shipment Delay Prediction for Cold Chain Distributor

company_profile.business_model: Temperature-controlled food distribution serving ~600 restaurant and grocery accounts

problem_statement: The distributor ran 240 refrigerated delivery routes daily but had no early warning system for delays. Drivers called dispatch when they fell behind, often with less than 30 minutes notice. Spoilage claims from late deliveries cost $1.4M annually, and restaurant clients were threatening contract cancellations after three consecutive holiday season failures.
solution_summary: Tenex built a delay prediction model that scored each active delivery every 15 minutes against predicted arrival time. The model used a gradient boosted classifier trained on GPS position, traffic data, weather, driver fatigue patterns (shift hours elapsed), and vehicle maintenance history. Dispatchers received alerts 2+ hours before predicted SLA breach — enough time to re-route, send a backup vehicle, or proactively call the client.

engagement_title: Fraud Detection for Mid-Market Payment Processor

company_profile.business_model: Payment processing platform serving ~4,500 SMB merchants; processes ~2M transactions per month

problem_statement: The processor's fraud system was a rules engine with 47 static rules, last updated 18 months prior. Fraud losses ran at 0.31% of transaction volume — above the industry benchmark of 0.18%. More damaging, the false positive rate of 3.2% was blocking legitimate transactions from merchants' best customers, generating chargeback disputes and merchant support tickets at a rate that required 6 FTE to manage.
solution_summary: Tenex built an ML fraud scoring layer that ran alongside the existing rules engine rather than replacing it. A gradient boosting model scored each transaction in under 80 milliseconds using 40 engineered features: velocity patterns, device fingerprints, geographic anomalies, merchant category context, and cross-merchant behavior. Scores above 0.85 were hard-blocked; scores 0.6–0.85 triggered a step-up challenge. The rules engine remained as a backstop.

primary_metric.label: Fraud caught rate
primary_metric.value: 31% more fraud intercepted vs rules-only baseline
primary_metric.baseline: 0.31% fraud loss rate on $240M annual transaction volume ($744K annual fraud losses)
primary_metric.outcome: 0.21% fraud loss rate ($504K annual losses — $240K annual avoidance)

secondary_metrics.label: False positive rate
secondary_metrics.value: Reduced from 3.2% to 1.1% of transactions

secondary_metrics.label: Chargeback dispute volume
secondary_metrics.value: Down 44% in 90 days post-launch

secondary_metrics.label: Fraud ops FTE redeployed
secondary_metrics.value: 3 of 6 FTE redeployed to product work within 6 months

results.measurement_basis: A/B test against rules-only control group (20% of traffic) for first 30 days; then full production comparison to prior quarter

engagement_details.client_team_involvement: 1 fraud ops analyst as domain expert, 1 backend engineer for API integration

tech_stack.ml_approach: XGBoost classifier with 40 engineered features; real-time scoring via REST API; daily model retraining on 7-day rolling window
infrastructure: AWS SageMaker for training and serving
Redis for velocity feature cache (sub-10ms lookups)

lessons_learned: Running ML alongside the rules engine (not replacing it) was the right call — it de-risked the launch and gave the fraud team confidence to gradually expand the ML decision boundary.

engagement_title: Credit Risk Scoring for Regional Business Lender

company_profile.business_model: Non-bank business lender offering term loans and lines of credit to SMBs; ~$300M loan book

problem_statement: The lender's credit decision process relied on a scorecard built in 2018 using 12 variables from credit bureau pulls. Underwriters spent 45–60 minutes per application manually reviewing bank statements, tax returns, and industry data. engagement_title: AML Transaction Monitoring for Community Bank

company_profile.business_model: Community bank with $1.8B in assets, 22 branches, focus on commercial and small business banking

problem_statement: The bank's AML system generated 1,200–1,400 alerts per month from rules-based transaction monitoring. Compliance analysts spent 70% of their time investigating alerts that turned out to be false positives — primarily salary payments, rent, and recurring vendor payments flagged by legacy rules. Three analysts were spending their entire work week on alert review with no time for higher-value compliance work. Regulatory pressure was increasing.
solution_summary: Tenex built an ML alert triage layer that scored each alert for true positive probability before it reached a compliance analyst. A random forest model used 35 features: transaction context, counterparty relationship history, account behavior baseline, and time-series deviation from normal. High-confidence false positives (score < 0.15) were auto-closed with audit trail; medium scores queued for expedited review; high scores were escalated immediately.

primary_metric.outcome: 416 alerts/month requiring review — 884 auto-closed per month with audit trail

secondary_metrics.value: Reduced from 18 days to 7 days average

secondary_metrics.label: Analyst capacity freed
secondary_metrics.value: Equivalent of 2.1 FTE recaptured for higher-value compliance work

secondary_metrics.label: True positive rate preserved
secondary_metrics.value: No reduction in SAR filing rate — same number of true cases identified

engagement_title: Clinical Triage NLP for Urgent Care Network

company_profile.business_model: Urgent care clinic network operating 28 locations; ~1.2M patient visits annually

problem_statement: Patients arriving at clinics completed paper intake forms that nursing staff manually reviewed to assign triage priority. Average time from patient arrival to triage assignment was 22 minutes, with significant variance depending on staff experience. High-acuity patients were not consistently prioritized, and the clinics faced patient satisfaction scores well below the network benchmark on wait time.
solution_summary: Tenex built an NLP-based triage assistance system that parsed patient intake text and symptom descriptions to generate an acuity score and flag red-flag indicators. A fine-tuned clinical BERT model classified chief complaints into ESI (Emergency Severity Index) levels 1–5 with confidence scores. The system displayed recommendations to triage nurses, who retained final clinical decision authority. It did not replace clinical judgment — it accelerated it.

secondary_metrics.value: Reduced by 11 minutes average for ESI 1-2 patients

secondary_metrics.label: Patient satisfaction (wait time item)
secondary_metrics.value: +14 points on post-visit survey

results.measurement_basis: EHR timestamps compared to 3-month baseline at same locations; patient satisfaction from post-visit survey

engagement_title: Readmission Risk Prediction for Regional Health System

company_profile.business_model: Regional health system operating 4 hospitals and 22 outpatient clinics; ~18,000 inpatient discharges annually

problem_statement: The health system's 30-day readmission rate was 14.8%, well above the national benchmark of 11.2% for their patient mix. CMS penalty exposure was $2.1M annually. Care managers did not have a risk stratification tool — they used clinical intuition to identify high-risk patients at discharge, which was inconsistent across units and shifts. High-risk patients who needed intensive post-discharge follow-up were not reliably identified before leaving the hospital.
solution_summary: Tenex built a readmission risk prediction model that scored every inpatient 24 hours before anticipated discharge. The model used 62 features from the EHR: diagnosis codes, comorbidities, medication burden, prior readmission history, social determinants of health (housing, transportation), and lab value trajectories. A gradient boosted model produced a 0–100 risk score with the top 5 risk factors. The care management team used scores to prioritize discharge planning conversations and post-discharge call schedules.

secondary_metrics.label: CMS penalty reduction
secondary_metrics.value: $1.4M reduction in annual CMS excess readmission penalties

secondary_metrics.label: Care manager time to high-risk patient contact
secondary_metrics.value: Reduced from 4.2 hours to 1.1 hours post-discharge

secondary_metrics.label: Model AUC on prospective validation
secondary_metrics.value: 0.78 — outperforming LACE score (0.64) used previously

results.measurement_basis: CMS readmission data; internal EHR readmission tracking; compared to prior 12-month period with patient mix adjustment

engagement_title: Prior Authorization Automation for HealthTech Platform

company_profile.business_model: HealthTech SaaS platform helping physician practices manage insurance prior authorizations; ~1,800 practice clients

problem_statement: The platform's 1,800 medical practice clients submitted prior authorization requests manually — staff typed clinical justifications into payer portals or faxed forms. Average time from request submission to approval was 4.2 days. Practices were losing 8–12% of prescribed procedures to authorization denials, many of which were administratively avoidable. The platform had the clinical data but no intelligence layer to help practices submit stronger, faster requests.
solution_summary: Tenex built an AI-assisted prior authorization drafting system that generated clinical justification narratives from structured EHR data. An LLM (Gemini Pro) was fine-tuned on 24 months of approved prior auth submissions across the platform's client base, learning which clinical arguments correlated with first-pass approval by payer and procedure type. The system generated a draft justification that practice staff reviewed and submitted — reducing the drafting step from 25 minutes to under 5 minutes.

primary_metric.baseline: 4.2 days average from submission to approval across all payers and procedure types
primary_metric.outcome: 18 hours average — 3.4-day reduction per authorization

secondary_metrics.value: Improved from 71% to 88% (17 percentage points)

secondary_metrics.label: Staff time per prior auth request
secondary_metrics.value: Reduced from 25 minutes to 4.5 minutes

secondary_metrics.label: Revenue recovered from previously denied procedures
secondary_metrics.value: Estimated $18K–$24K per 100-physician practice annually

results.measurement_basis: Platform authorization tracking data; payer response timestamps; first-pass vs appeal rate from payer portal logs

engagement_details.client_team_involvement: VP Product as executive sponsor, 2 product engineers for API integration, 1 clinical informatics advisor

engagement_title: Demand Forecasting for E-commerce Fulfillment Provider

company_profile.geography: US (3 fulfillment centers: Ohio, Nevada, Georgia)
company_profile.business_model: Third-party fulfillment provider handling warehouse, pick-pack, and shipping for ~180 e-commerce brands

problem_statement: The provider planned warehouse staffing weekly using a manual process: operations managers reviewed the prior week's order volume, applied a seasonal adjustment factor from a spreadsheet, and called the staffing agency with headcount requests. During peak seasons (November–December, Prime-like events), they consistently overstaffed in weeks before the peak and understaffed during the actual peak.
